text: The surgeon is positioned below the arcuate line, visible on the right side of the screen, and opens the attenuated posterior rectus sheath to access the preperitoneal pocket. The transverse abdominus muscle is clearly visible within this pocket.

The surgeon navigates within the preperitoneal space, staying close to the transverse abdominus muscle and avoiding the yellowish preperitoneal fat. The approach involves cutting the posterior rectus sheath near the arcuate line as part of the RoboTAR procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects to create a large pocket in the preperitoneal space, using the preperitoneal fat to gently peel the peritoneum away from the posterior rectus sheath, facilitating a safer incision of the sheath.

The surgeon dissects cephalad in the lateral space, with the grasp positioned behind the posterior rectus sheath. The arcuate line is visible, along with the linea semilunaris, which is formed by the posterior and anterior lamellae of the internal oblique.

The surgeon points out the linea semilunaris, a visible seam in the surgical field. After creating a pocket, the surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and the neurovascular bundle to avoid cutting through them. The posterior rectus sheath is then prepared for incision following a systematic checklist.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to separate the peritoneum from the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring it is not injured. The peritoneum is visibly close to the sheath, requiring precise dissection. As the dissection progresses cranially to the arcuate line, the separation becomes easier.

The surgeon creates a pocket and carefully peels the peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath. They check the linea semilunaris and locate the neurovascular bundle to avoid cutting across it. The posterior rectus sheath is then cut approximately one centimeter medial to the linea semilunaris, ensuring the peritoneum is detached.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, maintaining a clear plane between the peritoneum and the transverse salus fascia beneath the transverse abdominis. The peritoneum is carefully peeled off, creating a large pocket while ensuring visibility of the linea semilunaris and neurovascular bundles before cutting medial to these structures.

The surgeon creates a large pocket by separating the peritoneum from the posterior rectus sheath. As the dissection progresses higher, the layers of the posterior rectus sheath, including the posterior lamella, internal oblique, and transverse abdominis, visibly separate.

The surgeon demonstrates lateral dissection, showing the transverse salus fascia beginning to separate from the transverse abdominis. As the dissection progresses laterally, multiple fatty layers become visible. The surgeon stays close to the peritoneum, leaving more fat against the abdominal wall to avoid injuring the ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves.

The surgeon identifies a neurovascular bundle situated between the internal oblique and transverse abdominis muscles. The posterior lamina of the internal oblique is opened to lateralize the nerves and vessels, revealing the muscular structure of the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon encounters difficulty as the peritoneum adheres more firmly, making it challenging to sweep off. Despite this, the surgeon proceeds through the two layers of the posterior lamellar, internal oblique, and transverse abdominis, opting for a top-down approach for efficiency.

The surgeon transitions to a top-down approach for dissection, revealing minimal aponeurotic posterior rectus sheath in the patient. The Madrid-modified TAR technique is employed, leaving the muscle intact without dividing any muscle.

In the upper abdomen near the xiphoid process, the surgeon navigates into a plane with visible areolar tissue behind the posterior rectus sheath. Muscle fibers are observed on the floor, indicating the diaphragm's costal fibers. The surgeon carefully dissects beneath these fibers to avoid entering the incorrect space between the diaphragm and posterior rectus sheath, aiming for the plane between the muscle and transversalis fascia. Vessels entering the diaphragm muscle are cauterized to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon develops the space between the diaphragm and the posterior rectus sheath, dividing the white aponeurotic tissue to separate the peritoneum from these structures. The target dissection plane is between the transversalis fascia and the transverse abdominis, with efforts to become more superficial.

The surgeon divides aponeurotic tissue while observing the diaphragm muscle on the roof and taking down the transversalis attachments. Fat, identified as watershed fat, becomes visible, indicating proximity to the junction of the diaphragm and transverse abdominis muscles at the costal margin. The surgeon aims to position behind the transverse abdominis muscle to initiate the TAR procedure in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon continues the transversus abdominis release (TAR) by cutting through the posterior lamella, internal oblique, and transverse abdominus muscle. The dissection progresses laterally, maintaining the plane between the transverse abdominus and transversalis fascia, meeting the previously established dissection from the bottom-up approach.

The surgeon uses the scissor shimmying technique to carefully navigate between the transversalis fascia and the transversus abdominis, ensuring not to remain between the transversalis fascia and the peritoneum. The surgeon applies tension to develop the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon uses electrocautery on the transverse abdominus muscle to separate the transverse salus fascia from it, applying tension with the left hand to minimize the risk of perforating the transverse fascia and peritoneum. The peritoneum is held away to provide a broad width of tissue under tension, allowing for broader cauterization.

The surgeon uses cautery on the muscle side to address interdigitating fibers of the transverse salus fascia between the transverse abdominus muscle, carefully managing small blood vessels to prevent bleeding during dissection.

The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) to facilitate closure of the posterior layer in an ETEP case, aiming for anterior myofascial advancement due to the patient's small torso and large defect. As the dissection progresses laterally, the surgeon finds a clear plane, making the procedure easier.

The surgeon mobilizes the posterior layer, including the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath, to close a large gap in the posterior layer. As the dissection progresses laterally, the surgeon stays closer to the peritoneal side, leaving fat behind to avoid injuring nerves near the psoas muscle. Cautery is used extensively to manage small retracting vessels and prevent bleeding. The dissection extends under the diaphragm to accommodate a large piece of mesh for hernia repair, ensuring adequate overlap.

The surgeon achieves good hemostasis, allowing clear visualization of the surgical plane and rapid progression using cautery to separate tissue layers. Fat is left against the abdominal wall as the posterior layer is mobilized, preparing for closure.
